speaker: interviewee
text: When they made us work so hard, my sister, Hannah, and me, and I said to her that I don't want to live, I want to die. And who wants to live? They say if you touch the wire here, it's electricity. And one day I ran to the wires, I said I had enough already from life. And she started to scream, 'my sister wants to kill herself!'

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Polish girls who were lurking there, not the Germans, beat me so hard that they threw me in the hospital. My hand was broken. And they threw me in the hospital, and I got separated from my sister that time, Hannah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I was still in Auschwitz when my cousin heard that they took away, and they-- because she was very thin. That she's no good for work. And then I came back. No, I didn't come back from the hospital. Monday I thought that Hilda is dead, too, my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you think that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they took her away because she was very thin. They select her from the roll. You, you are no good for work anymore. So-- and we thought, whom they took away most of the time, they put them in the gas chamber. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know how I saw her that time, coming with a man's shoe and a man's coat, and with red and yellow stockings. And I thought that I see a ghost. I says, is that you, Hildegaard? I don't believe it's you. And she says to me, it's me. I says, where have you been? I've been in the gas chamber. It's just they didn't have the gas that time. So I-- they kept me there for three days, she said to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They stopped the gas. That's the way she survived it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How long were you in the hospital for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long they kept me, because after that I never met my sister. We were separated. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I met her when I got liberated from the Americans. Then I met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- was there anything else that happened in Auschwitz that comes to your memory before your liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz, like I say, in Auschwitz, we were constantly just standing outside and they counted us. From 5 o'clock in the morning, it was dark already-- they still counted us. And no more in the kitchen. I didn't work in the kitchen no more. No work and nothing. Just to be on the barracks there and dreaming for-- from food, most of the time. That was the main discussion. If we would have food, [BACKGROUND NOISE] what would be a different life. But it wasn't. No food at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other kinds of things that happened in Auschwitz? Were there any celebrations or special moments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Music was constantly playing. Some kind of music.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Germans were playing music/

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Some kind of music, constantly. You could hear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were talking with the inmates, it was the same sad story. Just waiting for dead. When they will kill us? Because you couldn't stay without food like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they brought to us to that barrack in a pail, a filthy pail, every day, some soup, that you looked at it, you got sick to your stomach. And that cousin of mine begged me, you must taste it if you want to survive and go home from here. She says, my brother is home. One of her brothers remained. I don't know how she knew it. Anyway, she forced me that I should have a swallow from that soup every day. Because I didn't want even to taste it, because my stomach couldn't take it. And she said to me, it's delicious. You will see. Yes, it was delicious. Filthy, and it taste what you give for horses. That was the smell of it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any humor there at all? Any humor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Humor? Humor. [LAUGHS] No humor. Just some of us hoped to wake up one morning and we will be free from here. That was the only thing. And just dreaming about food, about a piece of bread. I always said, if I would have a piece of bread, I'd give my whole life away right now. But it wasn't-- nobody wanted to buy my life. [SOBBING] [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] [? Now ?] talking about food, it made you lots of time feel fat, full in your stomach, because we constantly cooked what I never knew before. [LAUGHS] Everybody was cooking. I promised if I get out from here, every Sunday I will eat a raw potato. [LAUGHS] I didn't keep my promise. Just once or twice I tried. [LAUGHS] And I says, I can't do that. [PAUSES FOR 29 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it end?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had even tattooed there, I forgot to tell you all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They took us and they gave us tattoo that we are all right for work, I guess, because they done that for people who were working. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] My sister and I went together, Hannah, and she got the tattoo, too, the same way like me. We were always dreaming, or talking if we had a chance, that what happened to the rest of the family who came with us and everything. We didn't want to believe that they were burned. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working. They took me from the hospital there, separate from my sister. And they took me to a city-- Liebstadt. And Liebstadt, where I was working in a factory making parts of guns there. But it wasn't too many refugees. Most of the people were there were-- were prisoners-- French, and-- and Russian, and all kind of nationalities. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they were in the second barracks. We were in the first barracks there. The Jewish prisoners-- I mean, Jewish refugees-- and the war veterans, a lot of them from around the world. Turkish people that I never understood them. A lot of Russians and French people. And they were free Sunday. They always went to the city and brought sometime a carrot or a half a potato they gave us. Then it was a real holiday, if you could get something from them. But nobody ever would eat the carrot by themselves. We shared it right away with the other Jewish refugees. Once somebody gave me a soap. A piece of soap, not [LAUGHS] a big soap. Oh, I'm telling you that was the best thing in the world that we could wash ourselves a little bit. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I must tell you that thing because it's very-- how faith is. The war was on, and the American people were fighting still. And one day they were bombing and nobody got hurt in my barracks. And the war veterans, the bomb fell down on them. And all the people who were there, they were dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were so close to each other-- like two rooms from here. And we were talking, and I says, it can't be. How come that they all died? It just fell on their barracks. It could have happened to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And that was before the war ended already. And when we worked in that factory, and already they knew, the German, that is their end. They took us out from the factory. And three days they walked us around, like that, in circles. No places to go-- just in circles, with dogs close to us and with the German-- how, how should you say, who watches you. They watched us, and just in circles they walked us around. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Without food, we couldn't go anymore. A lot of people fell down there in between the walk and they couldn't get up. Then or they shoot them, or they beat them [INAUDIBLE]. Who still could go, was going around. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I did that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was April the first. We heard bombs constantly, close to us. But we stood in the barracks. They didn't take us out no places. We stood at night when the things came-- the bombs came. But after the three days that we went in circles, all of a sudden we heard tanks coming in. And, like crazy people, run to the tanks, all of us. Stop the American people to come in with the tanks and everything. We run in front of them. They thought that we are all from a whorehouse, or crazy without hair. And on our coat, everybody had a yellow-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. How you say? The Jewish star.

speaker: interviewee
text: Painted Jewish star. So they thought-- they didn't see. The first time I saw that time a colored guy in my life [LAUGHS] when they came in with the tanks. And we said, we are Jews. They didn't believe us. They thought that we are crazy, because it was-- that was April the first. And the, the war got ended April the 15, so they didn't believe us. I don't even know what they thought about us. But anyway, they gave us [? matzo ?] and they said, it's pizza. They gave us even cigars and cigarettes and everything, food to eat, canned goods. A lot of us became very ill. I smoked even a cigar. [LAUGHS] In my life, I never smoked even a cigarette. Everything to try. We were all crazy who were liberated-- not normal. We didn't know what we doing. And they were so nice to us. They didn't scream that we stop them with the tanks. It was the war, and they had to go. And we stopped the tanks-- hold on to them. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us all kind of canned, canned goods, SPAM. And what happened after that, the American people said we should go. No. We saw, first, when we walked around, we saw the white flag all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started to cry that I don't want to live because nobody's alive. I remain here alone-- no relatives, nobody. I-- How can I kill myself because I don't want to go? Where should I go if I don't have where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they took us to a little village and put us in the Germans' houses down there. And they had chickens running around there. And they had food, naturally, they didn't want to give us. Their pantry was full with-- how should I say, desserts. What they put away for the winter-- peaches, and all kinds of things. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And hanging meats. But they didn't want to give us those things. You need some more water? I'm sorry. Excuse me. Were you there with anybody that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Completely different people. You know, there were a lot of French people with high heels because they put them from the street. They picked them up that time already. But I was with one girl, a Hungarian. Her name was Magda. She came from not far from my city, from Kosice. I was with her, but most of them were French people that I couldn't speak to them at all. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Hannah, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: They separated me, right away. After that, they beat me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: [COUGHS] Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they hurt my hand. I was in the hospital, and they took her away. I just met her late, later.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet up with her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I was liberated by the Americans. And it was there, a chaplain, a rabbi. You say chaplain, yeah? And he brought a book with him from Belzenberg with the names who got liberated there from the English army, because there were English army, not American.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he brought a book with names. And I was sitting and looking the names to find some of my relatives. And I find Hannah Schoenfeld, and I started to scream. My sister! My sister! Where is she? My sister is there! I was screaming that she's alive. Where is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that chaplain took me cross Germany-- it was all over bombed-- in a Jeep, to find my sister in Belzenberg. And I came there. I was clean, already, from the American. I could wash myself, and I had a dress on from there. I don't know who gave me-- the Germans, or who gave me, I don't know. But I know I had a dress on, and I looked half-human.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he heard that I was screaming, my sister, where is she? Is she alive? That's my sister! So he says, yes, she's in Belzenberg. I want to see her. How could I see her? Is she coming here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I take you there. And he put me in his Jeep and he took me to where she was. But it was a long way-- I think, two days to go there. We had to go in that Jeep. And then I came to Belzenberg, what it was a-- horses were there. It was a-- I can't say it in English. What it was-- a stable with horses! And it was clean, the whole yard there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I met some people there from my hometown. And I said to them, where is my sister? Yes, she's here. She's alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I bent down. I was walking between half-dead people and dead people, because they were just laying. Most of them got food and got cholera. Sick, they were very sick. And I was walking between them and tried to pick up the faces to see which is my sister. And that's when I find my sister. And that chaplain took me back to [? Kaunitz, ?] where I was. And my sister was 80 pounds, skin and bone. And when she touched me I was afraid from her. I said to her, please, Hannah, don't touch me. Don't touch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid from her. She looked like somebody would come out from the grave. Not human at all. And I was so afraid of her. And when we went back, the chaplain took me back to the farm there. All the way that he took us back, my sister was so sick-- throwing up and making BM. And the chaplain cleaned her up-- everything, I'm telling you. I just looked at her. And when we came back to the farmhouse there, one day I went out and I stole a chicken. But I couldn't kill the chicken. I asked my friend, kill it and cook it that I should give for Hannah to eat some soup. Look how sick she is. And the Germans said to me that I stole from them the chicken. I wasn't afraid from them that time at all, because the Americans were there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and I fed my sister, spoon by spoon, she should eat something. And when I met my sister, she was, her hair was all full of-- not lice, not living lice, the dead lice. And I said, Hannah, how come? What happened to you that you look like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says to me, they put on me lice to see how much they can [INAUDIBLE] more. How many lice would be born on me.
